title: Am I supposed to like the characters in NGE
display_title: Am I supposed to like the characters in NGE
wikitext:
[]: Am I supposed to like the characters in NGE?             Quote Post by NotAnAnimeFan » Fri Mar 16, 2012 9:30 am Serious question. I'm an outsider to the anime/otaku fandom, so perhaps I'm coming from the wrong perspective here, but I'm genuinely confused by the way characters develop in the series. Okay, I absolutely loved NGE. I watched it because friends said it had excellent action sequences, and was primarily a bildungsroman (growing-up story, more or less) about Shinji, and I'm a sucker for that kind of story (Catcher in the Rye, Taxi Driver, Notes From Underground, etc) so I figured it was worth a shot. I had a blast watching it, and actually enjoyed Ep 26's exploration of existential concepts, and ultimately I agree with the decision Shinji made. So I immediately Google my way to a NGE forum to lurk for a few days, and I'm surprised to see how devoted fans are to characters. People say stuff like "Asuka is the best!" and "I LOVE Misato, why didn't she get more screen time?!" and "Rei Ayanami is so great!" I liked everything about NGE *except* its characters. In fact, I think that the show succeeds partially because its characters are unlikeable. Most of them are severely flawed to the point of chronic unhappiness and unapproachability, but that reinforces themes of dystopia and decay the establish the setting. Let's take a closer look. Ikari Shinji is the protagonist of the series, mainly because his father and mother were involved in Seele, which led to Yui becoming Eva 01, so that Shinji could pilot it. Basically, he is the hero because Gendo planned it that way for convenience's sake. Oh, yeah, [s]his dad killed his mom[/s] OKAY SO THAT'S NOT ACTUALLY TRUE BUT IT ALMOST IS (IT'S AN OEDIPUS COMPLEX I SWEAR) when he was two years old and now he's living full-time with a grown woman (Hi, Freud). He spends, like, 23 episodes lamenting the fact that he has to make hard decisions and face the consequences they bring, often willing to die rather than take responsibility for bad things (Hi, Kierkegaard, you and Dostoevsky can join Sigmund over here). The only time he responds to life courageously is in the last 10 minutes of the series (which were admittedly running low on cash). Eh, it was a bit deus ex machina for my tastes, but he finally does grow in the end. Still, I spent 95% of the show wishing he'd do something other than turtle up in his tapedeck. Ikari Gendo. I'll admit his character design is cool as hell, and I really enjoyed watching him take charge of Nerv in the fight against Unit 03- he was the only one willing to make the difficult decisions, or else the only one who could see that they were necessary. I always figured that in the end, it would turn out that he always had Shinji's best interests in mind, even when it really REALLY didn't seem like it, similar to Snape at the end of Harry Potter or something. But then it turned out he sort of didn't give a shit about much except being with Yui the whole time- at all, really. Maybe that was one of those deconstructions the series is so famous for. He says he was distant from his son because he didn't think he could do much except hurt him, but he doesn't seem particularly remorseful about it either. And then, even once Instrumentality starts, he kind of fucks off and hangs out with Yui, apparently - he barely makes an appearance in EoE or EPs. 25/26. In retrospect, we see he was selfish all along, and I don't like him either. Asuka Langley. She's introduced as a prodigy and an amazing Eva pilot, but past the underwater fight, all she does is suck at stuff and then get mad about how she sucks at stuff and then transfer her frustration onto every other character in a manner that is completely obvious to absolutely everyone except Shinji. I mean, what is she even good at? Math? Chemistry? Hopscoth? Also, shouldn't she be intelligent enough to see that Shinji's success in battle is always due to berserker mode or the dummy plug? Furthermore, the effectiveness of an Eva pilot depends almost entirely on what soul is in the Eva and what mood the pilot is in at the time. She repeats this cycle of behavior ad nauseam for, like, TWENTY TWO episodes, and in the original ending, never overcomes it. In EoE (the real ending, to be fair), she finally breaks free, and after dying in battle, courageously joins Shinji outside of Instrumentality (I'm pretty sire.) So that's cool - but we're still talking about maybe twenty minutes of a work spanning about nine hours. I still can't say that I like her character, because I haven't been shown any traits of hers that actually make her likeable. She's still a bitch, just a brave one now. I'm out of time now, gonna add more when I can. Also, I should say that Kaji and Yui (Fuyutsuki too, I guess?)are the few characters that I don't feel this way about. [/b]

[]:             Quote Post by Na7e » Fri Mar 16, 2012 10:31 am The characters were meant to be sympathetic. And, you really messed up. Gendo didn't kill Yui. Seele found out her plans and chose to go into Unit-01 to have some part of their scenario. And, Shinji not acting courageously Sachiel, Shamshel, Sandalphon, and Zeruel kind of counter that. And, the fact that his dad abandons him after his mom dies is going to cause problems. Gendo his hard decisions for Unit-03 weren't even necessary, he had three Eva's against one. And, he resorted to using the untested dummy plug, thank god this was only time he was in charge. Asuka there's a thread regarding her. Asuka: What was Anno thinking And, you do realize she is the most broken character in the show IMO. But, calling her a bitch is missing the point. She does good at piloting until the 13th. And, really you do realize that her entire personality is a band-aid, and she hates herself. Her bitchiness is designed to push people away, because she's deathly afraid their going to hate her or abandon her.

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Fri Mar 16, 2012 10:49 am It's true that most of the characters are generally unlikeable, but that's also a part of what makes them so appealing. For many, it's easy to relate to some of these characters and instantly connect with their plights. After that, it becomes a lot easier to see the good things about the characters, and you grow to love them.

[]:             Quote Post by Warren Peace » Fri Mar 16, 2012 11:11 am ^ This. Intrigue > virtue. In stories, the only unforgivable sin is to be boring.

[]:             Quote Post by Fireball » Fri Mar 16, 2012 11:52 am It helps greatly to relate to when you have similar backgrounds. Eva is a mirror for all those who went through depressions once. I don't hold it against anyone who doesn't like them though one should not ignore the reasons what made them so messed up. They all become a bit more sympathetic once you realize this.

[]:             Quote Post by Ornette » Fri Mar 16, 2012 12:46 pm I'm in the same boat, don't really like any of the characters and can't relate to any of them at all, but they do make for a great story.

[]:             Quote Post by the_seventh_child » Fri Mar 16, 2012 1:05 pm You're not supposed to like the characters but your analysis is shallow; it lacks depth and you have measured wrongly what makes them who they are.

[]: Re: Am I supposed to like the characters in NGE?             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Fri Mar 16, 2012 1:46 pm A lot of the NotAnAnimeFan wrote: "Asuka is the best!" and "I LOVE Misato, why didn't she get more screen time?!" and "Rei Ayanami is so great!"

[]:             Quote Post by driftking18594 » Fri Mar 16, 2012 4:57 pm I personally like some of the characters. As some other users have already pointed out, the characters are meant to be sympathised rather than liked. You can dislike Asuka but still have some shred of sympathy for her, for example. You can think Shinji is a whiny little shit but realise that you've probably been in his shoes before.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Fri Mar 16, 2012 5:11 pm I really don't understand this notion of characters (or people in general) being "unlikable". IME most people have some pretty serious failings working for them. They aren't as exaggerated as those of the kids in NGE, but they're still there. If you fixate on the failings they become unlikable, but if you can look past them they're just human. The only unlikable people I've encountered are either mentally unbalanced (and thus people I can't connect with or even meaningfully interact with) or people who have surrendered to their worldview to such an extent that they think it's okay to hurt other people and take pains to justify doing so (i.e. racists, homophobes, and the like who know they're taking steps to hurt other human beings but just don't care). In NGE I think the only people I found truly unlikable were the members of SEELE, and to some extent Gendo, Yui, and the Akagis (though Gendo and Yui were effectively trapped in a corner and the Akagis were more victims than anything else, so I find it hard to hold anything against them). The rest of the cast gets a pass on account of circumstances and batshit insane plotting by SEELE and the others.

[]:             Quote Post by Xana » Fri Mar 16, 2012 6:05 pm NotAnAnimeFan wrote: and I'm surprised to see how devoted fans are to characters. People say stuff like "Asuka is the best!" and "I LOVE Misato, why didn't she get more screen time?!" and "Rei Ayanami is so great!"

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Sat Mar 17, 2012 3:20 pm First off: WELCOME! It's always great to have a new fan! Did you check out the Rebuild movies? So, on to your post. Of course, the philosophy and plot are also important parts of what makes the story great, and it wouldn't be EVA without them. And telling the audience to grow the fuck up has indeed been one of the Author's main goals. But from what I've seen so far, the characters tend to be what ppl like best about the show/fascinates them the most. Even their makers were surprised by their popularity (Director: I don't klnow how the show got so popular! Everyone is sick!... Then again, the guy has about Shinji's level of self-esteem, he tends to underestimate his own Genius.) Of course, they are all far from perfect, but y'know, the best characters are not made by trying to make the audience like them - in fact, that's a perfect recipe for a Mary Sue. The best characters are made by giving them the traits that telling your story and getting your message across requires, and worrying about audience reaction later. What makes the characters so popular is exactly that they're not perfect - they are slightly stilized/overdrawn/metaphorical versions of types of people that our society actually produces, so the audience tends to recognize themselves in there - poor Shinji gets a little much hate because he is at times far too clear a mirror image (But this seems to be a mostly american phenomenon because of the, in my personal opinion, pretty skewered ideal of manliness they have there). But most of the time, ppl see themselves in there, recognize their own suffering in the character (which makes some ppl, probably including me, get very protective) and really learn something from watching that character's journey unfold that teaches them a lesson for their own lives, which makes them get even more attached. For me, it was mostly Rei, Mari, and partially, Misato and Shinji, but all characters really discuss and/or display real life behaviors... that show changed my view of the world and made me erase several phrases from my vocabulary. Also, there is just so much detail put into them, down to philosophic opinions. They're complex, multi-layered, with conflicting drives and interlinked aspects, full blown inner universes. We get shown so much of them, and yet, much is left in the dark - Even Shinji, you know, the PoV character/protagonist, has some mayor blank spots and vague allusions in his backstory. - and they all form parts of a whole, interact on many levels, represent different things in themselves and in the other cast members. All this simply tends to invite interest and fascination. I must say that even if there are some characters that I absolutely hate on an emotional level(namely, ), there is not one that I would want to miss, because I appreciate them all on an intellectual level, as ideas, parts of a piece of art. They manage to be intense and yet believable. What might make appreciating them a little hard is that few of them know their own value - thats part of the message, that your own idea of yourself/point of view influences more than you think, but if the characters were right in their low opinions of themselves, then they would have conducted their actions based on a correct fundament, and there would be nothing to teach them. This is part of the key to understanding stuff (Because, while you're free to dislike them, I don't think your interpretations of them are correct... in some cases, there's even official materials that say something else, even tough EVA is a show that ppl have very diverging, very strong, and very personal opinions about): The characters know much less than you do, and can't see all that you see, and that's only realistic. Neither Asuka nor Shinji have any clues about what influences their synch rations, and while we get to see the insides of most characters, they don't and that's part of the point. What makes people so passionate about either loving or hating them, is that they combine highly virtuous traits with fatal flaws or impedements. Another point is that character developement in this show is, in many cases (i.e. all characters who have large enough a role to be dynamic and don't just die...), not linear, but parabolic or S-shaped. It's been compared to a five-act drama: 1. We get introduced to the character, who either appears to be pretty idealized, is in a negative state, or, in most cases, both. both. 2. Stuff seems to be going well, if the character was in the negative category, they improve. We get to see more depht about them. 3. Things reach a climax, everything seems to be going perfectly... and then, they get their fingers stepped on, badly, and a combination of outside circumstances they had no controll over and their own, flawed reaction brings them down. 4. It gets worse, character proceeds to sink into the dephts of depair again , worse than before. The characters of the "idealized" variety get deconstructed, there's unsettling revelations. 5. Character comes to full bloom. This means either catastrophic end or solution. Now, let me discuss you interpretations in detail: Asuka What you just complained about is just about the point: Her story is that of a naive young girl getting shattered upon the hard rocks of reality, as much as she likes to act like she's the token adult. Shinji is the better fighter. (We'll get to that in a minute) and yeah, it isn't fair - many of the Angels just couldn't be beaten by hitting them with Knifes. She never really was that amazing , that's a facade, a complex she's got in her head, that she always needs to feel she's the best. It's not rational, so she won't think rationally about it and say "It's just luck/the dummy plug". They still need Shinji to stick him into EVA 01 for it to do anything - aparently, the dummy alone doesn't do the trick. That all he needs to to is sit in it with everything else happening pretty much by itself probably only made it more humiliating for poor Asuka. She always has that nagging voice in the bag of her head saying that she's not worth existing ("just like her mother"), and she's trying to shut it up with whatever satisfies it, and that is only a personal victory at her own hands, a lots of praise. She has the trauma that her mother never had a man's love and, like many children whose parents divorce, extends that to her. Her mother also neglected her in favor of her job, before she went mad and completely ignored her ever since, untill she killed herself, so Asuka feels really lonely and abandoned. In that way, she's a lost child that wants to be protected - but at the same time, she doesn't want to make the same mistakes as her mother and end up like her, so she doesn't want to depend on anyone, which creates her abrasive behavior, but contrasts with her need to be loved... as a result, almost all of her relationships become very much all take-no-give (She wants others to love her, but she doesn't want to love them ), futherly complicated by the fact that everyone is a rival by default and that she tends to project the things she hates about herself onto others, so she can hardly "see" them... and that makes her cruel. Of course, she is a hypocrite, self-centered and never stopps to consider what her behavior DOES to Shinji, Misato etc... no argument there, but she isn't that way because she's just a jerk... or well, maybe she is a jerk, but not an as one-dimensional-one as you think. , evben tough I personally don't think that excuses her. But her fans probably think it does. Ask some of them. Gendo "Forgive me, Shinji" *CRUNCH* = no remorse? Really? He DOES care. That's really the punchline, the beauty of the character: He DOES care, it's just that he happened to have exactly the same problem as Shinji. He lacked the confidence, he was so convinced that he would scew up that he didn't even try, and thought that sending Shinji far away from him was the best he could do for him. He thought that Shinji was the one who didn't want shit to do with him . Royal communication fail on both sides. All the time that Shinji spent wondering what his father was thinking, Gendo was probably wondering what Shinji was thinking... If you look closely, you will notice that the two of them actually start getting closer to each other over the course of the story, starting in episode 11 where Shinji sees that gendo's not just all talk, and Gendo apparently seemed to have trusted Shinji to arrive in time. Then, there goes ep 12, with that little word of praise. In ep 15, they actually have a conversation, and Shinji learns that Gendo's burning of Yui's stuff was more a grief reaction than just not needing it all anymore, he actually tries to offer Shinji some advice/ tells him not to blame himself for not remembering Yui... they actually talk about their feelings there, and Shinji tells Gendo that he actually likes spending time with him, which should be obvious to just everyone else but probably meant a lot to him. of course, only as much as they're capable of it, but still. In ep 16, we see that Shinji is deeply conflicted about it all because a few nice words can't just erase years of ignoring and he doesn't know the backstory there, but at the same time, he really wants it all to be true... Then, of course, come the doomy dooms of doom: For all their similarities, the main difference between the Ikaris is that Gendo is a rational person, while Shinji is more the emotional type - So when the Bardiel incident came, Gendo saw one alternative where everyone, including Shinji kicks the bucket for sure, and another version where just one person might kick the bucket, while Shinji just sees someone he doesn't want to hurt. after what transpired, Shinji says he feel "betrayed" and Gendo says he's "dissapointed", indicating that some trust had begun to exist there... but doesn't exist anymore. they basically give up on each other after that, both comming to the conclusion that the other hates them after all. Note how the shots where they both narrow their eyes in frustration directly parallel each other. In EoE, Gendo just dies regretfully, and that's the last we see of him, but for what it's worth, certain things in ep 26 suggest that stuff went a little differently in EoTV... Oh, one more thing: He is NOT the villain. That's still SEELE, and they hardly appear that much because deep black bad guys are just boring. He's one of the darker shades of grey, no argument there, but there's something you must understand about him: Remember how he justifies his actions in episode 25? "Because there is no other way to live.". Sure, he really, really wants his wife back, but that doesn't change that he simply has a very grim view of the world, and genuinely believes that his plan is the only future humanity has, and really wants to save the World from SEELE, even if most of us wouldn't consider that "saving.". he is doubtlessly misguided there, but he is no hypocrite: As you have noted, he's extremely pragmatic. He looks at the bottom line. He doesn't want to put his son and surrogate daugher in danger, and if he can avoid causaulities then... why not? but if it's either do A, whatever attrocity A may be, or else, poof end of the world, he'll do A. He tries to persuade Shinji to fight, but when that doesn't work, and if he doesn't use the dummy plug, everyone dies, including the unfortunate kid inside, then he'll use it. As you have correctly observed, he's the one who'll go and "just do something." But he applies that same cold logic to himself - if he has to do the dirty work himself, we will (ep 11) and he is, in fact, not above offering his own body to ppl if it serves his purpose, he's not that "arrogant, bossy guy with a god complex". Shinji *sigh* Why is he only ever remembered for the first few episodes and the first quarter of EoE? He thinks he is how you discribe him, but he really isn't. Indecisive? In the prologue arc, yes. Perhaps. Or maybe he didn't want to say the reasons for his decisions out loud. But he gets over that after episode 6. After that, he does plenty of eva-piloting without much complaint. Untill he decides that he has enough of it. But then he's told that the alternative is "poof, end of the world", after which he reconsiders that decision. Then, after a lot of shit happens, he decides to lay down and die and won't let himself be convinced otherwise. That was, of course, the wrong answer, but it isn't being indecisive. Yeah, he also tends to say he does stuff because others tell him to so he won't be to blame when stuff goes wrong... and you could call that "running away from responsibilities" without doing him much of an injustice, but that's only half the deal. He does that because he's sure that he will screw up. He really has a low opinion of himself, but doesn't grasp that that's just his point of view, his opinion whch he can change, no absolute truth. That's what happens in EoTV:He simply gets that, no deus ex machina involved, just good old philosophy there. In a way, he spends most the story with a really skewered view of the world. That's his problem, no argument here but... you know, that translates to: "The poor kid hates himself because he hasn't been hugged in his life" at least not before episode 16 or the second Rebuild movie. Of course, he could do better than that/ solve that problem, but... he's not the way he is because he's just a spoiled arrogant brat, dude. Also, while that skewered viewpoint of his colors his viewpoints and influences his answers a great deal, it doers that because it's narrow and incomplete - He's not that resignated, depressed victim just drifting along, he thinks he should be, maybe because it's easier, because having no expectations means he can't be dissapointed, but he's human, he does want, he does hope and he does get dissapointed. He's got some knot of bottled-up anger somewhere beneath the surface, something that makes him rather unpredictable.... his makers have disacribed him as irreformably stubborn, and the type of person who absolutely won't do what he doesn't want to do, who hasn't really done anything he didn't want in his life, which, does imply a certain determination there... it's just that he doesn't know. If he shifts the blame away, he'll never get the credit either, if he says he does what other's want, he won't realize what it is he wants... and finding that out, finding out who he really is is really a major part of that... journey he goes through. You say that he's "just the hero cuz of his parents"... but that's really the point: He didn't get picked because he passed some test in being heroic. He's just a fourteen-year old boy who is put into situations that could traumatize trained soldiers. Yeah, you're used to the classic "heroes" who quickly adapt to the situation, have an unshakeable moral backbone and proceed to kick ass and that's why they're heroes, but don't forget that those are exceptions . Sometimes, a person's ability to do something about a certain situation is influenced by their circumstances - You can't donate cash for children in Africa if you don't have any cash to donate. And not everyone who has caash is generous by default. So why should everyone whose mother happens to get absorbed into a mecha be a born hero? So, Shinji is a lonely fourteen-year-old in a war situation who acts a little more like an actual lonely fourteen-year-old in a war situation than your average fictional protagist. Of course, he still has to grow the fuck up, like any other insecure teenager, and the baggage from his father's abandonement doesn't exactly make it easy. He acts like a stubborn kid at times. But that's it: He's a teenager. Big sacrilege there. You say a teenager who can't handle getting thrown into a war and doesn't have the maturity to carry the weight of the world is an unlikeable, cowardly jerk. mind you, he does quite well, given the circumstances: His childhood was just as sucky as Asuka's and most prefer her because she always makes her complaints heard and manages to look s functional, but unlike her, he doesn't go around insulting people. Despite all the shit he's been through, he still spends most the time being a kind, polite, friendly, sensitive, at times quite pensive boy who just doesn't want to be hated. Also, he's pretty talented at various stuff like mecha-piloting, cooking (not being a macho is always a plus point) and music (Hidden dephts!) He spent the first few episodes "whining" because he was all alone had been dragged into a war and he's just freakin fourteen, and yet, what are these episodes actually about? About how he manages to establish a home for himself, form a family-like bond with Misato, get himself some friends and finally accept his place/Job. By episode 7, he doesn't really "whine" anymore unless there's a distinct reason, he even gets a little snarky there, and we see him acting within the margins of what would still count as an average boy. (At least untill everything he knows and loves starts being wrested from his grasp one by one... We'll get to that later) So, you say his decision in EoE was the only time he ever did anything noble and/ or courageous? Personally, I remember a liiiiiiitle bit more, like -Taking an injured girl into his arms / instantly running to check on her -Agreeing to risk his life for some girl he doesn't know -Risking his ass to protect some guy who beat him up earlier that day - charging at some big monster he is absolutely terrified of like some mad berserker, and keeping going after the thing had put some holes into something he was synchronized with (That wasn't exactly the most reasonable thing he ever did, but it was his first real battle, and it MUST take some guts.) - not escaping the prospect of getting further holes punched into him even tough all he'd have to do was to step into a train that was in front of his nose - asking himself why some poor chick is all alone - He was just as lonely a few days ago, and he didn't forget it. - After having just been grilled, he ultimately agrees to risk being grilled again so poor Rei doesn't have to fight the monster all alone. Yeah, he did complain/says he didn't want to/was afrait of it at first but... he was almost boiled alive and he's just 14. - hitting Ramiel when he had to fire at it manually - saving Rei's life and being nice to her. - going into the vincinity of something that was just about to have a nucluear meltdown. "Stop running you bastard!" - Episode 8... where to begin. While it's mostly remembered for him being insulted a lot, getting slapped twice... or was it thrice? and being forced into girl's clothes, few remember that this is actually one of his cooler moments - He seemed to be, like, the only taking that battle seriously! - Jumping into a freakin volcano to save yet another pretty girl! - Saving two pretty girls as well when they all fall down the shaft after Matarael's initial attack: It's Shinji who reacts fast enough to bring all three falling EVAs to a halt. - Being the only thing between the earth and Sahaquel. While we don't get as much glorious detail in Rebuild, EVa 01's arms DO explode in the original, as well. It's just that hardly anyone noticed cuz everything was so purple, which is probably why they dialed up the ketchup for Rebuild. I do have pics. - Cleaning Rei's appartment for her. - While he would've done the unfortunate pilot much more of a favor if he had properly fought Bardiel and tried to pull the entry plug out, it does show an, if misplaced, noble streak there that he'd rather die than extend his life at the cost of someone else's - more so when he initially wasn't aware that the fourth Child is someone he knows. - Threatening to trash headquarters - again, not exactly reasonable, and actually pretty childish, but nothing a coward would be able to pull off. (But yeah, hire monkeys, expect to pay in bananas. hire frustrated teenagers, expect teenage frustration) - He came back. Again. - Shinji vs. Zeruel. Just Shinji vs. Zeruel. Yes, he ultimately needed a little help from Mommy to actually beat it, but he basically had the strongest Angel (sans Kaworu) at his mercy before the power ran out at the worst possible moment. Asuka and Rei both had cables and were basically blown out of the way. - He could have stayed in EVA 01 and escaped all hartships of existence as a coherent bunch of molecules, but even if it took some promting for him to make a decicion, he did come back - He volunteered to fight a thing that rapes people's minds to save Asuka. It was probably a good idea to keep him in the cage, but it's the thought that matters - He also makes some admittedly rather counterproductive attempts at comforting her in that episode. - He pwns the remote-controlled EVA 02, off-screen, at that. With a knife to the head. Kaworu might or might not have been going easy on him, tough... - He shows that he has learned from the Bardiel fiasco, and is capable of "pulling the trigger" by the end of ep 24. It should be noted that these feats are made all the more admirable/ respectable because he's not your average fearless hero with that unshakeable belief that in what they're doing - it takes someone like Shinji much more effort, since he doesn't only have to overcome the dire circumstances at hand, which are already way beyond of what any kid his age should witness, he also has to overcome his own fears and limitations. His action in EoE were less than ideal, but he was really at his lowest there, this isn't his default state. You gotta forgive a fourteen-year-old who's been neglected for most of his life for snapping after being rejected by love interest A, watching his best friend get maimed, having his already barely< existant relationship with his father shatter for good, being liquified and put back together, having his father figure murdered, watching his mother figure falling into depression, getting some more rejection, watching love interest A get pretty much raped into insanity, having love interest B die in front of his eyes just after confessing that she "wants to be one with him", all of that without being able to do a thing, and then having to kill his new best friend with his own hands. Actually, he deserved some credid for not losing it much earlier.

[]:             Quote Post by the_seventh_child » Sat Mar 17, 2012 4:10 pm ^ My ass Shinji's better fighter than Asuka. Try Shinji without mama help and he got nothing, or at least nothing compared to her. She is by far superior. Also having graduated from college that early in age, kind of makes her a bit 'amazing'; at least when it comes to academics. Not gonna get into the rest of your point, as it is semi-subjective, but you can't ignore facts and declaring the opposite.

Not gonna get into the rest of your point, as it is semi-subjective, but you can't ignore facts and declaring the opposite.

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Sat Mar 17, 2012 5:47 pm the_seventh_child wrote: My ass Shinji's better fighter than Asuka. Try Shinji without mama help and he got nothing, or at least nothing compared to her. She is by far superior.

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Sat Mar 17, 2012 5:57 pm She might have had the training, but I say he still pwns her/must have overtaken her at some point, because while neither took Zeruel out on their own, one of them got substantially closer than the other. Tipp: It wasn't the one with advantages such as weapons, two functional arms on their EVA (at first) and a power cable. No doubt that she wins in the knowing-how-to-use-fancy equipment-and-actuallöy-having-some-clue-about-tactics respect, tough. Bagheera wrote: Asuka's struggle was less with the notion that she was somehow inadequate

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Sat Mar 17, 2012 5:59 pm Sachi wrote: I tend to agree that Asuka is the better pilot, but Shinji is equally capable in a different fashion. Asuka was trained for combat and knows how to operate her Eva efficiently and almost acrobatically, however Shinji can produce more offensive power due to his [s]synch ratio advantage[/s] mom and often receives better results. Different tactics, and his happened to work.

chunks.content:
Article: Am I supposed to like the characters in NGE
[]: Am I supposed to like the characters in NGE?             Quote Post by NotAnAnimeFan » Fri Mar 16, 2012 9:30 am Serious question. I'm an outsider to the anime/otaku fandom, so perhaps I'm coming from the wrong perspective here, but I'm genuinely confused by the way characters develop in the series. Okay, I absolutely loved NGE. I watched it because friends said it had excellent action sequences, and was primarily a bildungsroman (growing-up story, more or less) about Shinji, and I'm a sucker for that kind of story (Catcher in the Rye, Taxi Driver, Notes From Underground, etc) so I figured it was worth a shot. I had a blast watching it, and actually enjoyed Ep 26's exploration of existential concepts, and ultimately I agree with the decision Shinji made. So I immediately Google my way to a NGE forum to lurk for a few days, and I'm surprised to see how devoted fans are to characters. People say stuff like "Asuka is the best!" and "I LOVE Misato, why didn't she get more screen time?!" and "Rei Ayanami is so great!" I liked everything about NGE *except* its characters. In fact, I think that the show succeeds partially because its characters are unlikeable. Most of them are severely flawed to the point of chronic unhappiness and unapproachability, but that reinforces themes of dystopia and decay the establish the setting. Let's take a closer look. Ikari Shinji is the protagonist of the series, mainly because his father and mother were involved in Seele, which led to Yui becoming Eva 01, so that Shinji could pilot it. Basically, he is the hero because Gendo planned it that way for convenience's sake. Oh, yeah, [s]his dad killed his mom[/s] OKAY SO THAT'S NOT ACTUALLY TRUE BUT IT ALMOST IS (IT'S AN OEDIPUS COMPLEX I SWEAR) when he was two years old and now he's living full-time with a grown woman (Hi, Freud). He spends, like, 23 episodes lamenting the fact that he has to make hard decisions and face the consequences they bring, often willing to die rather than take responsibility for bad things (Hi, Kierkegaard, you and Dostoevsky can join Sigmund over here). The

Article: Am I supposed to like the characters in NGE
 fact that he has to make hard decisions and face the consequences they bring, often willing to die rather than take responsibility for bad things (Hi, Kierkegaard, you and Dostoevsky can join Sigmund over here). The only time he responds to life courageously is in the last 10 minutes of the series (which were admittedly running low on cash). Eh, it was a bit deus ex machina for my tastes, but he finally does grow in the end. Still, I spent 95% of the show wishing he'd do something other than turtle up in his tapedeck. Ikari Gendo. I'll admit his character design is cool as hell, and I really enjoyed watching him take charge of Nerv in the fight against Unit 03- he was the only one willing to make the difficult decisions, or else the only one who could see that they were necessary. I always figured that in the end, it would turn out that he always had Shinji's best interests in mind, even when it really REALLY didn't seem like it, similar to Snape at the end of Harry Potter or something. But then it turned out he sort of didn't give a shit about much except being with Yui the whole time- at all, really. Maybe that was one of those deconstructions the series is so famous for. He says he was distant from his son because he didn't think he could do much except hurt him, but he doesn't seem particularly remorseful about it either. And then, even once Instrumentality starts, he kind of fucks off and hangs out with Yui, apparently - he barely makes an appearance in EoE or EPs. 25/26. In retrospect, we see he was selfish all along, and I don't like him either. Asuka Langley. She's introduced as a prodigy and an amazing Eva pilot, but past the underwater fight, all she does is suck at stuff and then get mad about how she sucks at stuff and then transfer her frustration onto every other character in a manner that is completely obvious to absolutely everyone except Shinji. I mean, what is she even good at? Math? Chemistry? Hopscoth? Also, shouldn't she be intelligent enough to see that Shinji's success in battle is always due to berserker mode or the dummy plug? Furthermore, the effectiveness of an Eva pilot depends almost entirely on what soul is in the Eva and what mood the pilot is in at the time

Article: Am I supposed to like the characters in NGE
 to see that Shinji's success in battle is always due to berserker mode or the dummy plug? Furthermore, the effectiveness of an Eva pilot depends almost entirely on what soul is in the Eva and what mood the pilot is in at the time. She repeats this cycle of behavior ad nauseam for, like, TWENTY TWO episodes, and in the original ending, never overcomes it. In EoE (the real ending, to be fair), she finally breaks free, and after dying in battle, courageously joins Shinji outside of Instrumentality (I'm pretty sire.) So that's cool - but we're still talking about maybe twenty minutes of a work spanning about nine hours. I still can't say that I like her character, because I haven't been shown any traits of hers that actually make her likeable. She's still a bitch, just a brave one now. I'm out of time now, gonna add more when I can. Also, I should say that Kaji and Yui (Fuyutsuki too, I guess?)are the few characters that I don't feel this way about. [/b]

[]:             Quote Post by Na7e » Fri Mar 16, 2012 10:31 am The characters were meant to be sympathetic. And, you really messed up. Gendo didn't kill Yui. Seele found out her plans and chose to go into Unit-01 to have some part of their scenario. And, Shinji not acting courageously Sachiel, Shamshel, Sandalphon, and Zeruel kind of counter that. And, the fact that his dad abandons him after his mom dies is going to cause problems. Gendo his hard decisions for Unit-03 weren't even necessary, he had three Eva's against one. And, he resorted to using the untested dummy plug, thank god this was only time he was in charge. Asuka there's a thread regarding her. Asuka: What was Anno thinking And, you do realize she is the most broken character in the show IMO. But, calling her a bitch is missing the point. She does good at piloting until the 13th. And, really you do realize that her entire personality is a band-aid, and she hates herself. Her bitchiness is designed to push people away, because she's deathly afraid their going to hate her or abandon her.

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi

Article: Am I supposed to like the characters in NGE
 her entire personality is a band-aid, and she hates herself. Her bitchiness is designed to push people away, because she's deathly afraid their going to hate her or abandon her.

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Fri Mar 16, 2012 10:49 am It's true that most of the characters are generally unlikeable, but that's also a part of what makes them so appealing. For many, it's easy to relate to some of these characters and instantly connect with their plights. After that, it becomes a lot easier to see the good things about the characters, and you grow to love them.

[]:             Quote Post by Warren Peace » Fri Mar 16, 2012 11:11 am ^ This. Intrigue > virtue. In stories, the only unforgivable sin is to be boring.

[]:             Quote Post by Fireball » Fri Mar 16, 2012 11:52 am It helps greatly to relate to when you have similar backgrounds. Eva is a mirror for all those who went through depressions once. I don't hold it against anyone who doesn't like them though one should not ignore the reasons what made them so messed up. They all become a bit more sympathetic once you realize this.

[]:             Quote Post by Ornette » Fri Mar 16, 2012 12:46 pm I'm in the same boat, don't really like any of the characters and can't relate to any of them at all, but they do make for a great story.

[]:             Quote Post by the_seventh_child » Fri Mar 16, 2012 1:05 pm You're not supposed to like the characters but your analysis is shallow; it lacks depth and you have measured wrongly what makes them who they are.

[]: Re: Am I supposed to like the characters in NGE?             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Fri Mar 16, 2012 1:46 pm A lot of the NotAnAnimeFan wrote: "Asuka is the best!" and "I LOVE Misato, why didn't she get more screen time?!" and "Rei Ayanami is so great!"

[]:             Quote Post by driftking18594 » Fri Mar 16, 2012 4:57 pm I personally like some of the characters. As some other users have

Article: Am I supposed to like the characters in NGE
 "Rei Ayanami is so great!"

[]:             Quote Post by driftking18594 » Fri Mar 16, 2012 4:57 pm I personally like some of the characters. As some other users have already pointed out, the characters are meant to be sympathised rather than liked. You can dislike Asuka but still have some shred of sympathy for her, for example. You can think Shinji is a whiny little shit but realise that you've probably been in his shoes before.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Fri Mar 16, 2012 5:11 pm I really don't understand this notion of characters (or people in general) being "unlikable". IME most people have some pretty serious failings working for them. They aren't as exaggerated as those of the kids in NGE, but they're still there. If you fixate on the failings they become unlikable, but if you can look past them they're just human. The only unlikable people I've encountered are either mentally unbalanced (and thus people I can't connect with or even meaningfully interact with) or people who have surrendered to their worldview to such an extent that they think it's okay to hurt other people and take pains to justify doing so (i.e. racists, homophobes, and the like who know they're taking steps to hurt other human beings but just don't care). In NGE I think the only people I found truly unlikable were the members of SEELE, and to some extent Gendo, Yui, and the Akagis (though Gendo and Yui were effectively trapped in a corner and the Akagis were more victims than anything else, so I find it hard to hold anything against them). The rest of the cast gets a pass on account of circumstances and batshit insane plotting by SEELE and the others.

[]:             Quote Post by Xana » Fri Mar 16, 2012 6:05 pm NotAnAnimeFan wrote: and I'm surprised to see how devoted fans are to characters. People say stuff like "Asuka is the best!" and "I LOVE Misato, why didn't she get more screen time?!" and "Rei Ayanami is so great!"

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Sat Mar 17, 2012 3:20 pm First off:

Article: Am I supposed to like the characters in NGE
, why didn't she get more screen time?!" and "Rei Ayanami is so great!"

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Sat Mar 17, 2012 3:20 pm First off: WELCOME! It's always great to have a new fan! Did you check out the Rebuild movies? So, on to your post. Of course, the philosophy and plot are also important parts of what makes the story great, and it wouldn't be EVA without them. And telling the audience to grow the fuck up has indeed been one of the Author's main goals. But from what I've seen so far, the characters tend to be what ppl like best about the show/fascinates them the most. Even their makers were surprised by their popularity (Director: I don't klnow how the show got so popular! Everyone is sick!... Then again, the guy has about Shinji's level of self-esteem, he tends to underestimate his own Genius.) Of course, they are all far from perfect, but y'know, the best characters are not made by trying to make the audience like them - in fact, that's a perfect recipe for a Mary Sue. The best characters are made by giving them the traits that telling your story and getting your message across requires, and worrying about audience reaction later. What makes the characters so popular is exactly that they're not perfect - they are slightly stilized/overdrawn/metaphorical versions of types of people that our society actually produces, so the audience tends to recognize themselves in there - poor Shinji gets a little much hate because he is at times far too clear a mirror image (But this seems to be a mostly american phenomenon because of the, in my personal opinion, pretty skewered ideal of manliness they have there). But most of the time, ppl see themselves in there, recognize their own suffering in the character (which makes some ppl, probably including me, get very protective) and really learn something from watching that character's journey unfold that teaches them a lesson for their own lives, which makes them get even more attached. For me, it was mostly Rei, Mari, and partially, Misato and Shinji, but all characters really discuss and/or display real life behaviors... that show changed my view of the world and made me erase several phrases from my vocabulary. Also, there is just so much detail put into them, down to philosophic opinions. They're complex, multi-layered,

Article: Am I supposed to like the characters in NGE
/or display real life behaviors... that show changed my view of the world and made me erase several phrases from my vocabulary. Also, there is just so much detail put into them, down to philosophic opinions. They're complex, multi-layered, with conflicting drives and interlinked aspects, full blown inner universes. We get shown so much of them, and yet, much is left in the dark - Even Shinji, you know, the PoV character/protagonist, has some mayor blank spots and vague allusions in his backstory. - and they all form parts of a whole, interact on many levels, represent different things in themselves and in the other cast members. All this simply tends to invite interest and fascination. I must say that even if there are some characters that I absolutely hate on an emotional level(namely, ), there is not one that I would want to miss, because I appreciate them all on an intellectual level, as ideas, parts of a piece of art. They manage to be intense and yet believable. What might make appreciating them a little hard is that few of them know their own value - thats part of the message, that your own idea of yourself/point of view influences more than you think, but if the characters were right in their low opinions of themselves, then they would have conducted their actions based on a correct fundament, and there would be nothing to teach them. This is part of the key to understanding stuff (Because, while you're free to dislike them, I don't think your interpretations of them are correct... in some cases, there's even official materials that say something else, even tough EVA is a show that ppl have very diverging, very strong, and very personal opinions about): The characters know much less than you do, and can't see all that you see, and that's only realistic. Neither Asuka nor Shinji have any clues about what influences their synch rations, and while we get to see the insides of most characters, they don't and that's part of the point. What makes people so passionate about either loving or hating them, is that they combine highly virtuous traits with fatal flaws or impedements. Another point is that character developement in this show is, in many cases (i.e. all characters who have large enough a role to be dynamic and don't just die...), not linear, but parabolic or S-shaped. It's been compared to a five-act drama: 1. We get

Article: Am I supposed to like the characters in NGE
 many cases (i.e. all characters who have large enough a role to be dynamic and don't just die...), not linear, but parabolic or S-shaped. It's been compared to a five-act drama: 1. We get introduced to the character, who either appears to be pretty idealized, is in a negative state, or, in most cases, both. both. 2. Stuff seems to be going well, if the character was in the negative category, they improve. We get to see more depht about them. 3. Things reach a climax, everything seems to be going perfectly... and then, they get their fingers stepped on, badly, and a combination of outside circumstances they had no controll over and their own, flawed reaction brings them down. 4. It gets worse, character proceeds to sink into the dephts of depair again , worse than before. The characters of the "idealized" variety get deconstructed, there's unsettling revelations. 5. Character comes to full bloom. This means either catastrophic end or solution. Now, let me discuss you interpretations in detail: Asuka What you just complained about is just about the point: Her story is that of a naive young girl getting shattered upon the hard rocks of reality, as much as she likes to act like she's the token adult. Shinji is the better fighter. (We'll get to that in a minute) and yeah, it isn't fair - many of the Angels just couldn't be beaten by hitting them with Knifes. She never really was that amazing , that's a facade, a complex she's got in her head, that she always needs to feel she's the best. It's not rational, so she won't think rationally about it and say "It's just luck/the dummy plug". They still need Shinji to stick him into EVA 01 for it to do anything - aparently, the dummy alone doesn't do the trick. That all he needs to to is sit in it with everything else happening pretty much by itself probably only made it more humiliating for poor Asuka. She always has that nagging voice in the bag of her head saying that she's not worth existing ("just like her mother"), and she's trying to shut it up with whatever satisfies it, and that is only a personal victory at her own hands, a lots of praise. She has the trauma that her mother never had a man's love and, like many children whose parents divorce,

Article: Am I supposed to like the characters in NGE
's trying to shut it up with whatever satisfies it, and that is only a personal victory at her own hands, a lots of praise. She has the trauma that her mother never had a man's love and, like many children whose parents divorce, extends that to her. Her mother also neglected her in favor of her job, before she went mad and completely ignored her ever since, untill she killed herself, so Asuka feels really lonely and abandoned. In that way, she's a lost child that wants to be protected - but at the same time, she doesn't want to make the same mistakes as her mother and end up like her, so she doesn't want to depend on anyone, which creates her abrasive behavior, but contrasts with her need to be loved... as a result, almost all of her relationships become very much all take-no-give (She wants others to love her, but she doesn't want to love them ), futherly complicated by the fact that everyone is a rival by default and that she tends to project the things she hates about herself onto others, so she can hardly "see" them... and that makes her cruel. Of course, she is a hypocrite, self-centered and never stopps to consider what her behavior DOES to Shinji, Misato etc... no argument there, but she isn't that way because she's just a jerk... or well, maybe she is a jerk, but not an as one-dimensional-one as you think. , evben tough I personally don't think that excuses her. But her fans probably think it does. Ask some of them. Gendo "Forgive me, Shinji" *CRUNCH* = no remorse? Really? He DOES care. That's really the punchline, the beauty of the character: He DOES care, it's just that he happened to have exactly the same problem as Shinji. He lacked the confidence, he was so convinced that he would scew up that he didn't even try, and thought that sending Shinji far away from him was the best he could do for him. He thought that Shinji was the one who didn't want shit to do with him . Royal communication fail on both sides. All the time that Shinji spent wondering what his father was thinking, Gendo was probably wondering what Shinji was thinking... If you look closely, you will notice that the two of them actually start getting closer to each other over the course of the story, starting in episode 11 where Shinji sees that g

Article: Am I supposed to like the characters in NGE
, Gendo was probably wondering what Shinji was thinking... If you look closely, you will notice that the two of them actually start getting closer to each other over the course of the story, starting in episode 11 where Shinji sees that gendo's not just all talk, and Gendo apparently seemed to have trusted Shinji to arrive in time. Then, there goes ep 12, with that little word of praise. In ep 15, they actually have a conversation, and Shinji learns that Gendo's burning of Yui's stuff was more a grief reaction than just not needing it all anymore, he actually tries to offer Shinji some advice/ tells him not to blame himself for not remembering Yui... they actually talk about their feelings there, and Shinji tells Gendo that he actually likes spending time with him, which should be obvious to just everyone else but probably meant a lot to him. of course, only as much as they're capable of it, but still. In ep 16, we see that Shinji is deeply conflicted about it all because a few nice words can't just erase years of ignoring and he doesn't know the backstory there, but at the same time, he really wants it all to be true... Then, of course, come the doomy dooms of doom: For all their similarities, the main difference between the Ikaris is that Gendo is a rational person, while Shinji is more the emotional type - So when the Bardiel incident came, Gendo saw one alternative where everyone, including Shinji kicks the bucket for sure, and another version where just one person might kick the bucket, while Shinji just sees someone he doesn't want to hurt. after what transpired, Shinji says he feel "betrayed" and Gendo says he's "dissapointed", indicating that some trust had begun to exist there... but doesn't exist anymore. they basically give up on each other after that, both comming to the conclusion that the other hates them after all. Note how the shots where they both narrow their eyes in frustration directly parallel each other. In EoE, Gendo just dies regretfully, and that's the last we see of him, but for what it's worth, certain things in ep 26 suggest that stuff went a little differently in EoTV... Oh, one more thing: He is NOT the villain. That's still SEELE, and they hardly appear that much because deep black bad guys are just

Article: Am I supposed to like the characters in NGE
, certain things in ep 26 suggest that stuff went a little differently in EoTV... Oh, one more thing: He is NOT the villain. That's still SEELE, and they hardly appear that much because deep black bad guys are just boring. He's one of the darker shades of grey, no argument there, but there's something you must understand about him: Remember how he justifies his actions in episode 25? "Because there is no other way to live.". Sure, he really, really wants his wife back, but that doesn't change that he simply has a very grim view of the world, and genuinely believes that his plan is the only future humanity has, and really wants to save the World from SEELE, even if most of us wouldn't consider that "saving.". he is doubtlessly misguided there, but he is no hypocrite: As you have noted, he's extremely pragmatic. He looks at the bottom line. He doesn't want to put his son and surrogate daugher in danger, and if he can avoid causaulities then... why not? but if it's either do A, whatever attrocity A may be, or else, poof end of the world, he'll do A. He tries to persuade Shinji to fight, but when that doesn't work, and if he doesn't use the dummy plug, everyone dies, including the unfortunate kid inside, then he'll use it. As you have correctly observed, he's the one who'll go and "just do something." But he applies that same cold logic to himself - if he has to do the dirty work himself, we will (ep 11) and he is, in fact, not above offering his own body to ppl if it serves his purpose, he's not that "arrogant, bossy guy with a god complex". Shinji *sigh* Why is he only ever remembered for the first few episodes and the first quarter of EoE? He thinks he is how you discribe him, but he really isn't. Indecisive? In the prologue arc, yes. Perhaps. Or maybe he didn't want to say the reasons for his decisions out loud. But he gets over that after episode 6. After that, he does plenty of eva-piloting without much complaint. Untill he decides that he has enough of it. But then he's told that the alternative is "poof, end of the world", after which he reconsiders

Article: Am I supposed to like the characters in NGE
 that, he does plenty of eva-piloting without much complaint. Untill he decides that he has enough of it. But then he's told that the alternative is "poof, end of the world", after which he reconsiders that decision. Then, after a lot of shit happens, he decides to lay down and die and won't let himself be convinced otherwise. That was, of course, the wrong answer, but it isn't being indecisive. Yeah, he also tends to say he does stuff because others tell him to so he won't be to blame when stuff goes wrong... and you could call that "running away from responsibilities" without doing him much of an injustice, but that's only half the deal. He does that because he's sure that he will screw up. He really has a low opinion of himself, but doesn't grasp that that's just his point of view, his opinion whch he can change, no absolute truth. That's what happens in EoTV:He simply gets that, no deus ex machina involved, just good old philosophy there. In a way, he spends most the story with a really skewered view of the world. That's his problem, no argument here but... you know, that translates to: "The poor kid hates himself because he hasn't been hugged in his life" at least not before episode 16 or the second Rebuild movie. Of course, he could do better than that/ solve that problem, but... he's not the way he is because he's just a spoiled arrogant brat, dude. Also, while that skewered viewpoint of his colors his viewpoints and influences his answers a great deal, it doers that because it's narrow and incomplete - He's not that resignated, depressed victim just drifting along, he thinks he should be, maybe because it's easier, because having no expectations means he can't be dissapointed, but he's human, he does want, he does hope and he does get dissapointed. He's got some knot of bottled-up anger somewhere beneath the surface, something that makes him rather unpredictable.... his makers have disacribed him as irreformably stubborn, and the type of person who absolutely won't do what he doesn't want to do, who hasn't really done anything he didn't want in his life, which, does imply a certain determination there... it's just that he doesn't know. If he shifts the blame away, he'll never get

Article: Am I supposed to like the characters in NGE
 he doesn't want to do, who hasn't really done anything he didn't want in his life, which, does imply a certain determination there... it's just that he doesn't know. If he shifts the blame away, he'll never get the credit either, if he says he does what other's want, he won't realize what it is he wants... and finding that out, finding out who he really is is really a major part of that... journey he goes through. You say that he's "just the hero cuz of his parents"... but that's really the point: He didn't get picked because he passed some test in being heroic. He's just a fourteen-year old boy who is put into situations that could traumatize trained soldiers. Yeah, you're used to the classic "heroes" who quickly adapt to the situation, have an unshakeable moral backbone and proceed to kick ass and that's why they're heroes, but don't forget that those are exceptions . Sometimes, a person's ability to do something about a certain situation is influenced by their circumstances - You can't donate cash for children in Africa if you don't have any cash to donate. And not everyone who has caash is generous by default. So why should everyone whose mother happens to get absorbed into a mecha be a born hero? So, Shinji is a lonely fourteen-year-old in a war situation who acts a little more like an actual lonely fourteen-year-old in a war situation than your average fictional protagist. Of course, he still has to grow the fuck up, like any other insecure teenager, and the baggage from his father's abandonement doesn't exactly make it easy. He acts like a stubborn kid at times. But that's it: He's a teenager. Big sacrilege there. You say a teenager who can't handle getting thrown into a war and doesn't have the maturity to carry the weight of the world is an unlikeable, cowardly jerk. mind you, he does quite well, given the circumstances: His childhood was just as sucky as Asuka's and most prefer her because she always makes her complaints heard and manages to look s functional, but unlike her, he doesn't go around insulting people. Despite all the shit he's been through, he still spends most the time being a kind, polite, friendly, sensitive, at times quite pensive boy who just doesn't want to be hated. Also, he's pretty talented at various stuff like mecha-piloting, cooking (not

Article: Am I supposed to like the characters in NGE
 he still spends most the time being a kind, polite, friendly, sensitive, at times quite pensive boy who just doesn't want to be hated. Also, he's pretty talented at various stuff like mecha-piloting, cooking (not being a macho is always a plus point) and music (Hidden dephts!) He spent the first few episodes "whining" because he was all alone had been dragged into a war and he's just freakin fourteen, and yet, what are these episodes actually about? About how he manages to establish a home for himself, form a family-like bond with Misato, get himself some friends and finally accept his place/Job. By episode 7, he doesn't really "whine" anymore unless there's a distinct reason, he even gets a little snarky there, and we see him acting within the margins of what would still count as an average boy. (At least untill everything he knows and loves starts being wrested from his grasp one by one... We'll get to that later) So, you say his decision in EoE was the only time he ever did anything noble and/ or courageous? Personally, I remember a liiiiiiitle bit more, like -Taking an injured girl into his arms / instantly running to check on her -Agreeing to risk his life for some girl he doesn't know -Risking his ass to protect some guy who beat him up earlier that day - charging at some big monster he is absolutely terrified of like some mad berserker, and keeping going after the thing had put some holes into something he was synchronized with (That wasn't exactly the most reasonable thing he ever did, but it was his first real battle, and it MUST take some guts.) - not escaping the prospect of getting further holes punched into him even tough all he'd have to do was to step into a train that was in front of his nose - asking himself why some poor chick is all alone - He was just as lonely a few days ago, and he didn't forget it. - After having just been grilled, he ultimately agrees to risk being grilled again so poor Rei doesn't have to fight the monster all alone. Yeah, he did complain/says he didn't want to/was afrait of it at first but... he was almost boiled alive and he's just 14. - hitting Ramiel when he had to fire at it manually - saving Rei's life and being nice to her. - going into the

Article: Am I supposed to like the characters in NGE
/was afrait of it at first but... he was almost boiled alive and he's just 14. - hitting Ramiel when he had to fire at it manually - saving Rei's life and being nice to her. - going into the vincinity of something that was just about to have a nucluear meltdown. "Stop running you bastard!" - Episode 8... where to begin. While it's mostly remembered for him being insulted a lot, getting slapped twice... or was it thrice? and being forced into girl's clothes, few remember that this is actually one of his cooler moments - He seemed to be, like, the only taking that battle seriously! - Jumping into a freakin volcano to save yet another pretty girl! - Saving two pretty girls as well when they all fall down the shaft after Matarael's initial attack: It's Shinji who reacts fast enough to bring all three falling EVAs to a halt. - Being the only thing between the earth and Sahaquel. While we don't get as much glorious detail in Rebuild, EVa 01's arms DO explode in the original, as well. It's just that hardly anyone noticed cuz everything was so purple, which is probably why they dialed up the ketchup for Rebuild. I do have pics. - Cleaning Rei's appartment for her. - While he would've done the unfortunate pilot much more of a favor if he had properly fought Bardiel and tried to pull the entry plug out, it does show an, if misplaced, noble streak there that he'd rather die than extend his life at the cost of someone else's - more so when he initially wasn't aware that the fourth Child is someone he knows. - Threatening to trash headquarters - again, not exactly reasonable, and actually pretty childish, but nothing a coward would be able to pull off. (But yeah, hire monkeys, expect to pay in bananas. hire frustrated teenagers, expect teenage frustration) - He came back. Again. - Shinji vs. Zeruel. Just Shinji vs. Zeruel. Yes, he ultimately needed a little help from Mommy to actually beat it, but he basically had the strongest Angel (sans Kaworu) at his mercy before the power ran out at the worst possible moment. Asuka and Rei both had cables and were basically blown out of the way. - He could have stayed in EVA 01 and escaped all hartships of existence as a coherent bunch of molecules, but even

Article: Am I supposed to like the characters in NGE
 ran out at the worst possible moment. Asuka and Rei both had cables and were basically blown out of the way. - He could have stayed in EVA 01 and escaped all hartships of existence as a coherent bunch of molecules, but even if it took some promting for him to make a decicion, he did come back - He volunteered to fight a thing that rapes people's minds to save Asuka. It was probably a good idea to keep him in the cage, but it's the thought that matters - He also makes some admittedly rather counterproductive attempts at comforting her in that episode. - He pwns the remote-controlled EVA 02, off-screen, at that. With a knife to the head. Kaworu might or might not have been going easy on him, tough... - He shows that he has learned from the Bardiel fiasco, and is capable of "pulling the trigger" by the end of ep 24. It should be noted that these feats are made all the more admirable/ respectable because he's not your average fearless hero with that unshakeable belief that in what they're doing - it takes someone like Shinji much more effort, since he doesn't only have to overcome the dire circumstances at hand, which are already way beyond of what any kid his age should witness, he also has to overcome his own fears and limitations. His action in EoE were less than ideal, but he was really at his lowest there, this isn't his default state. You gotta forgive a fourteen-year-old who's been neglected for most of his life for snapping after being rejected by love interest A, watching his best friend get maimed, having his already barely< existant relationship with his father shatter for good, being liquified and put back together, having his father figure murdered, watching his mother figure falling into depression, getting some more rejection, watching love interest A get pretty much raped into insanity, having love interest B die in front of his eyes just after confessing that she "wants to be one with him", all of that without being able to do a thing, and then having to kill his new best friend with his own hands. Actually, he deserved some credid for not losing it much earlier.
